main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Gap-Aware Preference Optimization (GaPO): integrates estimated semantic gap (using metrics like ROUGE L, Jaccard Score, BERTScore) into the preference optimization loss function, replacing the traditional margin term with a gap score to better reflect human preference intensity.","Modification of the loss function in preference optimization to use a dynamic, metric-based gap between winning and losing examples, rather than a fixed margin.","Empirical evaluation of different gap estimation metrics and normalization techniques within the GaPO framework."],"problems":["Traditional RLHF methods use binary labels for preference pairs, failing to capture the nuanced, pairwise differences in human perception, which can degrade model alignment and performance.","Existing reward functions in preference optimization do not effectively compute or utilize sentence-level semantic gaps, leading to suboptimal gradient allocation and learning from preference data."],"datasets":["AlpacaEval 2.0"],"metrics":["Win Rate (on AlpacaEval 2.0)","ROUGE L","Jaccard Score","BERTScore"],"results":[{"metric":"Win Rate (AlpacaEval 2.0)","value":"52.8%"},{"metric":"Improvement over baseline methods (Win Rate)","value":"5.3 points higher"}],"novelty_claims":["GaPO introduces the concept of human preference intensity by estimating and incorporating semantic gaps between preference pairs into the optimization process, providing a more nuanced supervisory signal than binary labels.","The method replaces the traditional margin term in the loss function with a dynamically estimated gap based on general evaluation metrics, allowing for more rational gradient allocation and improved learning from preference data.","GaPO is empirically shown to outperform state-of-the-art methods (such as DPO and SimPO) on standard benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of gap-based preference optimization."]}

methods: Gap-Aware Preference Optimization (GaPO): integrates estimated semantic gap (using metrics like ROUGE L, Jaccard Score, BERTScore) into the preference optimization loss function, replacing the traditional margin term with a gap score to better reflect human preference intensity.
Modification of the loss function in preference optimization to use a dynamic, metric-based gap between winning and losing examples, rather than a fixed margin.
Empirical evaluation of different gap estimation metrics and normalization techniques within the GaPO framework.
problems: Traditional RLHF methods use binary labels for preference pairs, failing to capture the nuanced, pairwise differences in human perception, which can degrade model alignment and performance.
Existing reward functions in preference optimization do not effectively compute or utilize sentence-level semantic gaps, leading to suboptimal gradient allocation and learning from preference data.

results.value: 5.3 points higher

novelty_claims: GaPO introduces the concept of human preference intensity by estimating and incorporating semantic gaps between preference pairs into the optimization process, providing a more nuanced supervisory signal than binary labels.

The method replaces the traditional margin term in the loss function with a dynamically estimated gap based on general evaluation metrics, allowing for more rational gradient allocation and improved learning from preference data.
GaPO is empirically shown to outperform state-of-the-art methods (such as DPO and SimPO) on standard benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of gap-based preference optimization.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Weighted Preference Optimization (WPO): reweights off-policy preference pairs according to their probability under the current policy to simulate on-policy learning","Weighting alignment mechanism: ensures all on-policy generated pairs are equally weighted","Hybrid RL setting: combines off-policy preference data with on-policy outputs for optimization"],"problems":["Distributional gap between policy used for data collection and target policy in off-policy preference optimization","Suboptimal and inefficient optimization in off-policy RLHF due to treating all preference data equally, regardless of relevance to current policy","Need for cost-efficient and scalable preference optimization that matches on-policy RL performance"],"datasets":["Alpaca Eval 2","MT-bench","Ultrafeedback (as off-policy preference data source)"],"metrics":["Length-controlled winning rate against GPT-4-turbo","Improvement over SFT model","Improvement over DPO"],"results":[{"metric":"Length-controlled winning rate against GPT-4-turbo (Alpaca Eval 2, hybrid RL setting, Gemma-2-9b-it)","value":"76.7%"},{"metric":"Improvement over DPO on Alpaca Eval 2","value":"up to 5.6%"},{"metric":"Improvement over SFT model on Alpaca Eval 2","value":"up to 14.9%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes WPO, a new objective that reweights off-policy preference pairs by their probability under the current policy to simulate on-policy learning with off-policy data","Introduces a weighting alignment mechanism to ensure equal weighting of on-policy generated pairs","Demonstrates that WPO can be integrated into other preference optimization loss functions and consistently improves performance","Establishes new state-of-the-art results on Alpaca Eval 2 in the hybrid RL setting","Systematically analyzes the importance of on-policy, dispreferred data in preference optimization"]}

methods: Weighted Preference Optimization (WPO): reweights off-policy preference pairs according to their probability under the current policy to simulate on-policy learning
Weighting alignment mechanism: ensures all on-policy generated pairs are equally weighted
Hybrid RL setting: combines off-policy preference data with on-policy outputs for optimization
problems: Distributional gap between policy used for data collection and target policy in off-policy preference optimization
Suboptimal and inefficient optimization in off-policy RLHF due to treating all preference data equally, regardless of relevance to current policy
Need for cost-efficient and scalable preference optimization that matches on-policy RL performance

results.metric: Length-controlled winning rate against GPT-4-turbo (Alpaca Eval 2, hybrid RL setting, Gemma-2-9b-it)
Improvement over DPO on Alpaca Eval 2
results.value: up to 5.6%

results.metric: Improvement over SFT model on Alpaca Eval 2
results.value: up to 14.9%

novelty_claims: Proposes WPO, a new objective that reweights off-policy preference pairs by their probability under the current policy to simulate on-policy learning with off-policy data
Introduces a weighting alignment mechanism to ensure equal weighting of on-policy generated pairs
Demonstrates that WPO can be integrated into other preference optimization loss functions and consistently improves performance
Establishes new state-of-the-art results on Alpaca Eval 2 in the hybrid RL setting
Systematically analyzes the importance of on-policy, dispreferred data in preference optimization

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SimPO (Simple Preference Optimization): uses average log probability of a sequence as implicit reward, eliminates the need for a reference model, and introduces a target reward margin to the Bradley-Terry objective."],"problems":["Improving the simplicity and effectiveness of offline preference optimization in RLHF for large language models.","Addressing the misalignment between reward formulation and generation metric in DPO.","Reducing computational and memory overhead in preference optimization methods."],"datasets":["AlpacaEval 2","MT-Bench","Arena-Hard","Chatbot Arena (with real user votes)"],"metrics":["Length-controlled win rate (LC)","Raw win rate (WR)","Generation length","Leaderboard ranking"],"results":[{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2 Length-controlled win rate (Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"72.4%"},{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2 Raw win rate (Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"65.9%"},{"metric":"Arena-Hard win rate (Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"59.1%"},{"metric":"Improvement over DPO on AlpacaEval 2","value":"up to 6.4 points"},{"metric":"Improvement over DPO on Arena-Hard","value":"up to 7.5 points"},{"metric":"Chatbot Arena ranking (<10B models, Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"1st"}],"novelty_claims":["SimPO introduces a reference-free reward formulation using the average log probability of a sequence, eliminating the need for a reference model.","SimPO aligns the reward function with the generation metric, improving training effectiveness.","A target reward margin is introduced to the Bradley-Terry objective to encourage a larger margin between winning and losing responses.","SimPO is more compute and memory efficient than DPO and other reference-based methods.","SimPO achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models under 10B parameters."]}

methods: SimPO (Simple Preference Optimization): uses average log probability of a sequence as implicit reward, eliminates the need for a reference model, and introduces a target reward margin to the Bradley-Terry objective.
problems: Improving the simplicity and effectiveness of offline preference optimization in RLHF for large language models.
Addressing the misalignment between reward formulation and generation metric in DPO.
Reducing computational and memory overhead in preference optimization methods.

results.value: up to 6.4 points
up to 7.5 points
1st

novelty_claims: SimPO introduces a reference-free reward formulation using the average log probability of a sequence, eliminating the need for a reference model.
SimPO aligns the reward function with the generation metric, improving training effectiveness.
A target reward margin is introduced to the Bradley-Terry objective to encourage a larger margin between winning and losing responses.
SimPO is more compute and memory efficient than DPO and other reference-based methods.
SimPO achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models under 10B parameters.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["DPO-Positive (DPOP): a new loss function and training procedure for preference optimization","Theoretical analysis of DPO failure modes","Empirical evaluation of DPO and DPOP on various datasets","Creation and open-sourcing of Smaug-34B and Smaug-72B models"],"problems":["Failure mode of Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) where the likelihood of preferred examples can decrease during training","Performance degradation of LLMs when fine-tuned with DPO, especially on datasets with low edit distance between completions","General robustness and effectiveness of preference optimization methods for LLM alignment"],"datasets":["Common preference datasets for LLM fine-tuning (not named specifically in the excerpt)","Datasets with low and high edit distances between completions","MT-Bench","HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard"],"metrics":["Average accuracy (on HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard)","Performance on MT-Bench","Downstream task performance (e.g., reasoning, summarisation, alignment)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average accuracy (HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard)","value":"Smaug-72B surpasses 80% (first open-source LLM to do so)"},{"metric":"Performance on MT-Bench","value":"DPOP-tuned model outperforms DPO-tuned model (all else equal)"}],"novelty_claims":["Identification and theoretical analysis of a failure mode in standard DPO where the likelihood of preferred examples can decrease","Empirical demonstration that this failure mode occurs in practice, especially with low edit distance datasets","Proposal of DPO-Positive (DPOP), a new loss function and training procedure that avoids this failure mode","Demonstration that DPOP outperforms DPO and other fine-tuning procedures across a wide variety of datasets and tasks, including those with high edit distances","Creation and open-sourcing of Smaug-34B and Smaug-72B, with Smaug-72B being the first open-source LLM to surpass 80% average accuracy on the HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard"]}

methods: DPO-Positive (DPOP): a new loss function and training procedure for preference optimization
Theoretical analysis of DPO failure modes
Empirical evaluation of DPO and DPOP on various datasets
Creation and open-sourcing of Smaug-34B and Smaug-72B models
problems: Failure mode of Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) where the likelihood of preferred examples can decrease during training
Performance degradation of LLMs when fine-tuned with DPO, especially on datasets with low edit distance between completions
General robustness and effectiveness of preference optimization methods for LLM alignment
datasets: Common preference datasets for LLM fine-tuning (not named specifically in the excerpt)

Downstream task performance (e.g., reasoning, summarisation, alignment)

results.value: Smaug-72B surpasses 80% (first open-source LLM to do so)
DPOP-tuned model outperforms DPO-tuned model (all else equal)

novelty_claims: Identification and theoretical analysis of a failure mode in standard DPO where the likelihood of preferred examples can decrease
Empirical demonstration that this failure mode occurs in practice, especially with low edit distance datasets
Proposal of DPO-Positive (DPOP), a new loss function and training procedure that avoids this failure mode
Demonstration that DPOP outperforms DPO and other fine-tuning procedures across a wide variety of datasets and tasks, including those with high edit distances
Creation and open-sourcing of Smaug-34B and Smaug-72B, with Smaug-72B being the first open-source LLM to surpass 80% average accuracy on the HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard

novelty_claims: Introduction of Statistical Rejection Sampling Optimization (RSO) to sample preference pairs from the estimated optimal policy using rejection sampling, improving estimation accuracy.
Unified statistical framework for loss functions in SLiC and DPO, showing their relationship to SVM and logistic regression, and improving SLiC as the SVM counterpart of DPO.
Demonstration that explicit reward models (pairwise reward-ranking) are more effective for preference optimization than direct optimization on preference data.
Comprehensive recipe for loss functions and preference pair generation, showing the importance of the reward model and sampling strategy.

novelty_claims: First demonstration of applying Sequence Likelihood Calibration (SLiC) with human feedback (SLiC-HF) as an alternative to RLHF.
SLiC-HF can effectively use off-policy human feedback data collected for different models, reducing the need for new feedback collection.
SLiC-HF is simpler to implement, easier to tune, and more computationally efficient than RLHF-PPO, while achieving competitive or superior results.
Provides a general SLiC-HF recipe based on open-sourced T5 models that outperforms RLHF on the Reddit TL;DR summarization task.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Direct Preference Optimization (DPO): a simple RL-free algorithm for training language models from preferences using a binary cross-entropy objective","Comparison to PPO-based RLHF (Proximal Policy Optimization-based Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)"],"problems":["Difficulty in precisely controlling the behavior of large unsupervised language models","Complexity and instability of existing RLHF pipelines for aligning LMs with human preferences","High computational cost and implementation complexity of RLHF methods"],"datasets":["Human preference datasets for sentiment modulation","Human preference datasets for summarization","Human preference datasets for single-turn dialogue"],"metrics":["Sentiment control accuracy","Response quality (summarization and dialogue)","Computational efficiency (simplicity, stability, training cost)"],"results":[{"metric":"Sentiment control accuracy","value":"DPO exceeds PPO-based RLHF"},{"metric":"Response quality (summarization and dialogue)","value":"DPO matches or improves over PPO-based RLHF"},{"metric":"Computational efficiency","value":"DPO is substantially simpler to implement and train than PPO-based RLHF"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a new parameterization that allows direct optimization of language models from preference data without explicit reward modeling or reinforcement learning.","DPO enables extraction of the optimal policy in closed form using a simple binary cross-entropy loss, eliminating the need for sampling from the LM during fine-tuning and significant hyperparameter tuning.","DPO achieves performance equal to or better than existing RLHF methods while being more stable, performant, and computationally lightweight."]}

methods: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO): a simple RL-free algorithm for training language models from preferences using a binary cross-entropy objective
Comparison to PPO-based RLHF (Proximal Policy Optimization-based Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)
problems: Difficulty in precisely controlling the behavior of large unsupervised language models
Complexity and instability of existing RLHF pipelines for aligning LMs with human preferences
High computational cost and implementation complexity of RLHF methods
datasets: Human preference datasets for sentiment modulation
Human preference datasets for single-turn dialogue

results.value: DPO exceeds PPO-based RLHF
DPO matches or improves over PPO-based RLHF
DPO is substantially simpler to implement and train than PPO-based RLHF

novelty_claims: Introduction of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a new parameterization that allows direct optimization of language models from preference data without explicit reward modeling or reinforcement learning.

DPO enables extraction of the optimal policy in closed form using a simple binary cross-entropy loss, eliminating the need for sampling from the LM during fine-tuning and significant hyperparameter tuning.
DPO achieves performance equal to or better than existing RLHF methods while being more stable, performant, and computationally lightweight.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Absolute-Rating Multi-Objective Reward Model (ArmoRM) trained with multi-dimensional absolute-rating data","Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) strategy with a gating network (shallow MLP) to select and scalarize reward objectives based on context","Training ArmoRM with Llama-3 8B as the backbone"],"problems":["Lack of interpretability in reward models (RMs) used for aligning large language models (LLMs) via RLHF","Reward hacking and misalignment due to black-box nature of conventional RMs","Verbosity bias and other biases in RMs that do not reflect true human preferences"],"datasets":["RewardBench"],"metrics":["RewardBench overall score (benchmark for reward models)"],"results":[{"metric":"RewardBench overall score","value":"State-of-the-art (exact value not specified), surpassing LLM-as-a-judge with GPT-4 judges and approaching Nemotron-4 340B reward model"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a two-stage approach for interpretable reward modeling: (1) multi-objective reward modeling with absolute ratings, and (2) a mixture-of-experts gating network for context-dependent scalarization of objectives.","First to demonstrate that a multi-objective, interpretable RM (ArmoRM-Llama3-8B) can outperform LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4) and approach the performance of much larger models (Nemotron-4 340B) on RewardBench.","Introduces a method for decomposing RM outputs into human-interpretable objectives, enabling diagnosis and mitigation of biases such as verbosity bias.","Enables steerability of reward models by allowing adjustment of objective weights, improving alignment with human values."]}

methods: Absolute-Rating Multi-Objective Reward Model (ArmoRM) trained with multi-dimensional absolute-rating data
Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) strategy with a gating network (shallow MLP) to select and scalarize reward objectives based on context
Training ArmoRM with Llama-3 8B as the backbone
problems: Lack of interpretability in reward models (RMs) used for aligning large language models (LLMs) via RLHF
Reward hacking and misalignment due to black-box nature of conventional RMs
Verbosity bias and other biases in RMs that do not reflect true human preferences
metrics: RewardBench overall score (benchmark for reward models)

results.value: State-of-the-art (exact value not specified), surpassing LLM-as-a-judge with GPT-4 judges and approaching Nemotron-4 340B reward model

novelty_claims: Proposes a two-stage approach for interpretable reward modeling: (1) multi-objective reward modeling with absolute ratings, and (2) a mixture-of-experts gating network for context-dependent scalarization of objectives.

First to demonstrate that a multi-objective, interpretable RM (ArmoRM-Llama3-8B) can outperform LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4) and approach the performance of much larger models (Nemotron-4 340B) on RewardBench.
Introduces a method for decomposing RM outputs into human-interpretable objectives, enabling diagnosis and mitigation of biases such as verbosity bias.
Enables steerability of reward models by allowing adjustment of objective weights, improving alignment with human values.

novelty_claims: First to leverage MCTS for iterative, step-level preference data collection in LLM reasoning tasks, inspired by AlphaZero.
Combines outcome validation and stepwise self-evaluation to improve consistency and quality of intermediate steps in LLM reasoning.
Demonstrates that on-policy, step-level preference learning with MCTS and DPO leads to significant improvements over supervised fine-tuning baselines.
Provides analysis of training and inference compute tradeoff, showing more efficient performance gains compared to sampling-only approaches.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Odds Ratio Preference Optimization (ORPO): a monolithic, reference model-free preference alignment algorithm that appends a log odds ratio term to the negative log-likelihood loss during supervised fine-tuning (SFT)"],"problems":["Reducing the complexity and resource requirements of preference alignment in language models by eliminating the need for a reference model and multi-stage training","Improving the efficiency and performance of preference alignment for language models without requiring a separate SFT warm-up phase"],"datasets":["UltraFeedback","AlpacaEval 2.0","MT-Bench","IFEval"],"metrics":["AlpacaEval 2.0 score (percentage)","MT-Bench score","IFEval instruction-level loose accuracy (percentage)"],"results":[{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2.0 (Mistral-ORPO-α)","value":"11.33%"},{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2.0 (Mistral-ORPO-β)","value":"12.20%"},{"metric":"MT-Bench (Mistral-ORPO-α)","value":"7.24"},{"metric":"MT-Bench (Mistral-ORPO-β)","value":"7.32"},{"metric":"IFEval loose accuracy (Mistral-ORPO-α)","value":"61.63%"},{"metric":"IFEval loose accuracy (Mistral-ORPO-β)","value":"66.19%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a monolithic preference alignment method (ORPO) that does not require a reference model or a separate SFT warm-up phase, unlike previous methods such as RLHF and DPO.","Demonstrates that a minor penalty for the disfavored style during SFT is sufficient for effective preference alignment, simplifying the training process.","Shows that the odds ratio is a theoretically and empirically justified choice for contrasting favored and disfavored styles in SFT.","Achieves state-of-the-art or superior results on standard benchmarks (AlpacaEval 2.0, MT-Bench, IFEval) with a single training phase and smaller model sizes compared to larger, multi-stage aligned models."]}

methods: Odds Ratio Preference Optimization (ORPO): a monolithic, reference model-free preference alignment algorithm that appends a log odds ratio term to the negative log-likelihood loss during supervised fine-tuning (SFT)
problems: Reducing the complexity and resource requirements of preference alignment in language models by eliminating the need for a reference model and multi-stage training
Improving the efficiency and performance of preference alignment for language models without requiring a separate SFT warm-up phase
novelty_claims: Proposes a monolithic preference alignment method (ORPO) that does not require a reference model or a separate SFT warm-up phase, unlike previous methods such as RLHF and DPO.
Demonstrates that a minor penalty for the disfavored style during SFT is sufficient for effective preference alignment, simplifying the training process.
Shows that the odds ratio is a theoretically and empirically justified choice for contrasting favored and disfavored styles in SFT.

Achieves state-of-the-art or superior results on standard benchmarks (AlpacaEval 2.0, MT-Bench, IFEval) with a single training phase and smaller model sizes compared to larger, multi-stage aligned models.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Empirical analysis of length exploitation in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)","Development of a regularization strategy to control verbosity in DPO models"],"problems":["Length (verbosity) exploitation in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for language models","Bias in human preference data leading to longer but not necessarily higher quality responses","Lack of methods to control verbosity in DPO, unlike classical RLHF"],"datasets":["Anthropic’s Helpful and Harmless (HH) dialogue dataset","Reddit TL;DR summarization dataset","Alpaca Eval benchmark"],"metrics":["Win rate (including length-corrected win rate)","Average response length"],"results":[{"metric":"Win rate improvement (length-controlled DPO vs. unregularized DPO)","value":"up to 20% improvement"},{"metric":"Average response length (unregularized DPO vs. regularized DPO)","value":"Regularized DPO produces answers closer in length to SFT and human feedback distributions, while unregularized DPO produces answers twice as long on average"}],"novelty_claims":["First empirical study of length exploitation in DPO setting, showing it is prevalent and linked to out-of-distribution bootstrapping","Development of a simple, principled regularization strategy for DPO that controls verbosity without sacrificing model quality, even under length-biased evaluation","Demonstration that length-regularized DPO achieves significant improvements in win rates when controlling for length, despite evaluator bias"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Regression-based debiasing of automated evaluation metrics","Generalized linear model (GLM) to control for output length in auto-annotator preferences","Length-controlled AlpacaEval (AlpacaEval-LC): conditioning evaluation on zero length difference"],"problems":["Bias in LLM-based auto-annotators, specifically preference for longer outputs","Spurious correlations in automated evaluation metrics for open-ended NLP tasks","Lack of robustness in automated evaluation metrics to manipulations such as verbosity"],"datasets":["AlpacaEval benchmark","LMSYS Chatbot Arena"],"metrics":["Spearman correlation with LMSYS Chatbot Arena"],"results":[{"metric":"Spearman correlation with LMSYS Chatbot Arena (AlpacaEval)","value":"0.94"},{"metric":"Spearman correlation with LMSYS Chatbot Arena (AlpacaEval-LC)","value":"0.98"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a simple, interpretable regression-based debiasing strategy for automated evaluation metrics in NLP.","Introduces length-controlled AlpacaEval (AlpacaEval-LC), which conditions on zero length difference to remove length bias.","Demonstrates that AlpacaEval-LC achieves the highest reported correlation with human evaluation (Chatbot Arena) among automatic benchmarks.","Shows that the debiased metric is more robust to manipulations in model verbosity."]}

Generalized linear model (GLM) to control for output length in auto-annotator preferences
Length-controlled AlpacaEval (AlpacaEval-LC): conditioning evaluation on zero length difference
problems: Bias in LLM-based auto-annotators, specifically preference for longer outputs
Spurious correlations in automated evaluation metrics for open-ended NLP tasks

Spearman correlation with LMSYS Chatbot Arena (AlpacaEval-LC)

novelty_claims: Proposes a simple, interpretable regression-based debiasing strategy for automated evaluation metrics in NLP.
Introduces length-controlled AlpacaEval (AlpacaEval-LC), which conditions on zero length difference to remove length bias.
Demonstrates that AlpacaEval-LC achieves the highest reported correlation with human evaluation (Chatbot Arena) among automatic benchmarks.
Shows that the debiased metric is more robust to manipulations in model verbosity.

methods: Preference strength measurement based on multi-reward model voting
Label correction for incorrect preferences
Label smoothing for ambiguous preferences
Adaptive margin in loss function based on preference strength
Contrastive learning for reward modeling

Iterative RLHF with continuous reward model adaptation
problems: Incorrect and ambiguous preference pairs in RLHF datasets hinder accurate reward modeling
Poor generalization of reward models to out-of-distribution (OOD) examples
Instability and inefficiency in iterative RLHF training due to reward model limitations

results.metric: Alignment performance improvement (number of RLHF rounds)
results.value: Consistent improvement of the language model in 3 to 4 rounds on both HH-RLHF and OpenAI summarization datasets

novelty_claims: Propose a preference strength measurement metric based on multi-reward model voting to identify and mitigate incorrect and ambiguous preferences in RLHF datasets.
Introduce adaptive margin in the loss function based on preference strength to improve discrimination between similar responses.
Apply contrastive learning to reward modeling to enhance the model's ability to distinguish subtle preference differences and improve generalization.
Employ meta-learning to enable reward models to generalize to out-of-distribution data and support iterative RLHF training.
Demonstrate that the proposed methods lead to more stable RL processes and significant improvements in alignment performance.

methods: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of GPT-3 on human-written demonstrations of desired behavior
Training a reward model (RM) on human-labeled comparisons of model outputs
Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) using the PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) algorithm to fine-tune the model against the reward model
Mixing PPO updates with updates that increase the log likelihood of the pretraining distribution (PPO-ptx) to reduce performance regressions
problems: Large language models do not reliably follow user instructions and can generate untruthful, toxic, or unhelpful outputs (misalignment with user intent)
Standard language modeling objectives do not align models with the goal of being helpful, honest, and harmless
Performance regressions (alignment tax) on public NLP datasets when aligning models with human feedback
datasets: OpenAI API prompt distribution (labeler-written and user-submitted prompts)
metrics: Performance on public NLP datasets (e.g., SQuAD, DROP, HellaSwag, WMT 2015)

results.metric: Human labeler preference (1.3B InstructGPT vs 175B GPT-3)
results.value: 1.3B InstructGPT preferred over 175B GPT-3 despite 100x fewer parameters

results.metric: Human labeler preference (175B InstructGPT vs 175B GPT-3)
results.value: 85% ± 3% preferred InstructGPT

results.metric: Human labeler preference (175B InstructGPT vs few-shot 175B GPT-3)
results.value: 71% ± 4% preferred InstructGPT
InstructGPT generates truthful/informative answers about twice as often as GPT-3
InstructGPT: 21%, GPT-3: 41%

results.metric: Toxicity rate (RealToxicityPrompts, prompted to be respectful)
results.value: InstructGPT generates about 25% fewer toxic outputs than GPT-3

results.metric: Human labeler preference (InstructGPT vs T0 and FLAN on API prompt distribution)
results.value: InstructGPT winrate: 73.4% ± 2%; T0: 26.8% ± 2%; FLAN: 29.8% ± 2%

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that fine-tuning large language models with human feedback (RLHF) can significantly improve alignment with user intent, outperforming much larger models trained only with standard objectives.
Shows that models trained with RLHF (InstructGPT) are preferred by humans over much larger baseline models and over models fine-tuned on public NLP datasets (FLAN, T0).
Introduces a method (PPO-ptx) to reduce performance regressions on public NLP datasets while maintaining alignment improvements.
Provides evidence that RLHF-trained models generalize to new instructions and to preferences of labelers not seen during training.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Contrastive Preference Optimization (CPO)","Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) baseline","Curated preference data for training"],"problems":["Performance gap between moderate-sized LLMs (7B/13B) and state-of-the-art encoder-decoder MT models or larger LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) in machine translation","Limitations of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) due to quality issues in human-generated reference data","Lack of mechanisms in SFT to prevent near-perfect but flawed translations"],"datasets":["WMT'21 test set","WMT'22 test set","WMT'23 test set","FLORES-200 data (for analysis and training)"],"metrics":["wmt23-cometkiwi-da-xxl (reference-free)","XCOMET-XXL (reference-free)","wmt22-cometkiwi-da (reference-free)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average reference-free score (across 8 directions, WMT'22)","value":"ALMA-13B-R matches or surpasses GPT-4 and WMT competition winners (exact numbers in Appendix A)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Contrastive Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel training approach for LLM-based machine translation that avoids the limitations of SFT and reference-mimicking.","Demonstration that CPO-trained ALMA models (ALMA-R) can match or exceed the performance of state-of-the-art systems (including GPT-4 and WMT winners) using only 22K parallel sentences and 12M parameters.","Release of a high-quality preference dataset for machine translation.","In-depth analysis showing that human-written reference translations can be inferior to system-generated translations, challenging the reliance on reference-based evaluation."]}

methods: Contrastive Preference Optimization (CPO)
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) baseline
Curated preference data for training
problems: Performance gap between moderate-sized LLMs (7B/13B) and state-of-the-art encoder-decoder MT models or larger LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) in machine translation
Limitations of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) due to quality issues in human-generated reference data
Lack of mechanisms in SFT to prevent near-perfect but flawed translations

results.value: ALMA-13B-R matches or surpasses GPT-4 and WMT competition winners (exact numbers in Appendix A)

novelty_claims: Introduction of Contrastive Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel training approach for LLM-based machine translation that avoids the limitations of SFT and reference-mimicking.

Demonstration that CPO-trained ALMA models (ALMA-R) can match or exceed the performance of state-of-the-art systems (including GPT-4 and WMT winners) using only 22K parallel sentences and 12M parameters.
Release of a high-quality preference dataset for machine translation.
In-depth analysis showing that human-written reference translations can be inferior to system-generated translations, challenging the reliance on reference-based evaluation.

methods: RRHF (Rank Responses to align Human Feedback): a training paradigm that aligns model probabilities of multiple responses with human preferences using ranking loss on log probabilities of responses from various sources (model itself, other LLMs, human responses)
Sampling responses from various sources (model, ChatGPT, GPT-4, human experts) for training
Ranking loss to align model log probabilities with human preference orderings
Comparison with PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) and SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning)
problems: Complexity and resource requirements of PPO in RLHF for aligning language models with human preferences
Sensitivity of PPO to hyperparameters and need for multiple models during training
Need for a simpler, more scalable method to align language models with human feedback
datasets: Alpaca prompts and responses (for Wombat model evaluation)
metrics: Human labeling (human evaluation of helpfulness and harmlessness)

results.metric: Reward model score (Helpful and Harmless dataset)
results.value: RRHF achieves performance on par with PPO
RRHF achieves comparable alignment performance to PPO
RRHF outperforms SFT under similar training resources

novelty_claims: Proposes RRHF, a new learning paradigm for aligning language models with human feedback using ranking loss on log probabilities, requiring only 1-2 models during tuning (vs. 4 for PPO)
RRHF is simpler than PPO in terms of coding, number of models, and hyperparameter tuning, while achieving comparable performance
RRHF can leverage responses from various sources (including other LLMs and humans) for training
RRHF is an extension of SFT and reward model training, but more efficient and scalable than PPO
Demonstrates that RRHF is a best-of-n learner, with performance highly related to the quality of sampled responses

methods: RISE: an end-to-end imitation learning baseline that predicts continuous actions from single-view 3D point clouds
Sparse 3D encoder using sparse convolutions to compress point clouds into tokens
Sparse positional encoding to encode relative positions of 3D points for transformer input
Transformer-based feature extraction from point cloud tokens
Diffusion head to decode features into continuous robot action trajectories
problems: Sensitivity of image-based imitation learning policies to camera view changes
Inefficiency and lack of real-time performance in 3D point cloud-based policies for continuous action prediction
Difficulty in handling dynamic and contact-rich manipulation scenarios with keyframe-based 3D policies
Generalization and robustness of imitation learning policies to environmental changes (e.g., object arrangement, camera view, table height)
datasets: 6 real-world robot manipulation tasks (including pick-and-place, 6-DoF pouring, push-to-goal with tool, and long-horizon tasks) with 50 demonstrations per task
metrics: Robustness to environmental disturbance (camera view, table height)

results.value: RISE significantly outperforms other representative 2D and 3D policies by a large margin (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)
RISE keeps stable performance when the number of objects increases (qualitative)
RISE is more robust to changing camera views and increasing table height compared to baselines (qualitative)

novelty_claims: First end-to-end imitation learning baseline that predicts continuous actions directly from single-view 3D point clouds for real-world robot manipulation
Efficient sparse 3D encoder and sparse positional encoding for transformer-based policy learning from point clouds
Demonstrates significant improvements in accuracy, efficiency, generalization, and robustness over existing 2D and 3D imitation learning policies
